name: Ticketmaster Monopoly Full
description: The 'Ticketmaster Monopoly' theory posits that Ticketmaster, and its parent company Live Nation Entertainment, operates as a de facto monopoly in the live event ticketing industry in the United States, stifling competition, inflating ticket prices, and harming both artists and consumers. Proponents argue that through mergers, acquisitions, and exclusive contracts with venues, Ticketmaster controls a disproportionately large share of the market, giving it the power to dictate terms and conditions. This control extends to the resale market, further cementing their dominance. Evidence cited includes the high service fees charged on tickets, the difficulty independent venues face in securing acts, and the limited choice consumers have when purchasing tickets for popular events. Critics of this theory, while acknowledging Ticketmaster's significant market share, argue that the live entertainment industry is complex, with many factors influencing ticket prices and availability. They point to the role of artists in setting prices, the costs associated with operating venues, and the popularity of certain events as contributing factors beyond Ticketmaster's direct control. Furthermore, they argue that Ticketmaster provides a valuable service by streamlining the ticketing process and combating fraud. Mainstream economic analysis often focuses on market concentration and its potential impact on consumer welfare, with ongoing debates about whether Ticketmaster's practices constitute anticompetitive behavior or simply reflect the realities of a competitive, albeit concentrated, market. The debate often involves comparisons to other industries with high market concentration and the effectiveness of antitrust regulations in addressing such situations.
subtitle: Dominance in Live Event Ticketing

claims.claim: Ticketmaster controls a monopoly in the live event ticketing market.
claims.evidence: High market share, exclusive venue contracts, acquisition of competitors.
claims.criticism: Market share doesn't equal a monopoly; artists and venues also influence prices; the live event industry is complex.

claims.claim: Ticketmaster's practices lead to inflated ticket prices and excessive fees.
claims.evidence: High service fees, dynamic pricing (surge pricing), resale market control.
claims.criticism: Fees cover costs of ticketing services; dynamic pricing reflects demand; resale market exists independently.

claims.claim: Independent venues and artists are disadvantaged by Ticketmaster's dominance.
claims.evidence: Difficulty securing acts, lower profit margins, limited negotiating power.
claims.criticism: Venues and artists choose to work with Ticketmaster; alternative ticketing options exist; popularity drives act selection.

keyFigures.name: Pearl Jam
keyFigures.role: Banded together to sue Ticketmaster over unfair practices in the 1990s

keyFigures.name: Irving Azoff
keyFigures.role: Music executive with a long history with Ticketmaster, sometimes seen as a key figure in the industry's evolution.

keyFigures.name: Senator Amy Klobuchar
keyFigures.role: Has publicly criticized Ticketmaster's practices and called for greater regulation.

mythologyConnections: David vs. Goliath (struggle against a large corporation)
The Hydra (cutting off one head only for more to appear – referring to attempts to regulate the company)

timeline.event: Pearl Jam sues Ticketmaster over service fees and market dominance.
Ticketmaster merges with Live Nation to form Live Nation Entertainment.
Taylor Swift Eras Tour ticket sales debacle sparks renewed criticism and Congressional scrutiny.

sources.author: Dean Budnick
sources.work: Ticket Masters: The Rise of Live Nation and the Business of Concerts
sources.title: Dean Budnick

sources.author: United States Congress
sources.work: Hearing on Competition in the Live Entertainment Industry
sources.title: United States Congress
